channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. War Against Iran Enters a New Phase
item.description: As President Trump resumes his war, the focus is now on the Strait of Hormuz. But it remains unclear how far the U.S. military will go to exert control.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Eric Schmitt and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday. The latest phase of the U.S. military campaign in Iran has a new focus, but not necessarily a clearer strategy.

item.title: For Israel, the U.S.-Iran Hostilities Have Created an Uneasy Limbo
item.description: Officials see a return to full-blown war as preferable to an agreement that fails to curb the threats Iran poses to Israel. Meanwhile, they wait.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Israelis gathering near a public shelter in Tel Aviv after a warning of incoming missiles from Iran in March.

item.title: Trump Flip-Flops on Strait of Hormuz Plan, and U.S. Cyclospora Outbreak Spreads
item.description: Plus, Chipotle is coming to Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Ben Casselman and Glenn Thrush
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz carried about one-fifth of the world’s oil before the war, making it one of the most critical shipping lanes.

item.title: China’s Economy Grows at Slowest Pace in Years
item.description: Economic growth of 4.3 percent in the second quarter, versus the same period last year, reflected a broad slump outside of the country’s export-oriented manufacturing might.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shoppers at an electric car showroom in Shanghai. Chinese consumers have embraced a culture of thriftiness, as many are not feeling the benefits of economic growth.

item.title: China’s Chip Champion to Raise Billions in Race for A.I. Control
item.description: CXMT, a linchpin in China’s drive to develop homegrown artificial intelligence technology, is aiming to raise nearly $10 billion in a blockbuster public offering in Shanghai.
item.dc:creator: Steve Lohr and Xinyun Wu
item.media:credit: CFOTO/Future Publishing, via Getty Images)
item.media:description: ChangXin Memory Technologies in Hefei. Little known outside of China, the 10-year-old company is benefiting from booming demand for memory chips.

item.title: Colombian Immigrant Killed by ICE in Maine Had Legal Status, Father Says
item.description: The father of Joan Sebastian Guerrero, the man fatally shot by a federal immigration agent on Monday, said his son had been working two jobs to support his wife and daughter.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales, Jenna Russell and Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial for Joan Sebastian Guerrero at the intersection where he was shot by ICE in Biddeford, Maine.

item.title: Videos Detail Moments Surrounding Fatal ICE Shooting in Biddeford, Maine
item.description: Footage obtained by The New York Times shows ICE agents in the moments before and after the killing of Joan Sebastian Guerrero. The circumstances of the shooting remain unclear.
item.dc:creator: Allison McCann, Devon Lum, Tim Ouillette, Aric Toler and Leanne Abraham

item.title: An ICE Shooting in Maine Puts Pressure on Senator Susan Collins
item.description: The Democrats hoping to challenge Ms. Collins, a vulnerable Republican, have seized on the fatal incident, drawing attention to her record on immigration matters.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s disappointing to see potential candidates use a tragedy to try to advance a political agenda,” Senator Susan Collins told reporters at the U.S. Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: ICE Shootings Put Spotlight on Lack of Body Cameras
item.description: Kristi Noem, the former homeland security secretary, said five months ago that the Trump administration would “rapidly” deploy body cameras to immigration agents across the country. About half still don’t have them.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Joseph Prezioso/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers at the scene of the ICE shooting in Biddeford, Maine, on Tuesday.

item.title: Ukraine Pounds Russian Ships in Its Campaign to Cut Off Crimea
item.description: After striking roads and railways, Kyiv is now focusing on sea routes as it tries to disrupt fuel supplies and pressure Russia to end the war.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva, Marc Santora and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Commander of Unmanned Aerial Systems Force, via Reuters
item.media:description: A screenshot taken from footage provided by Ukraine’s Unmanned Systems Forces showed a drone flying toward a ship at port in the city of Kerch in Crimea in early July.

item.title: Cyclospora Cases Rise Rapidly, With No Source Yet Confirmed
item.description: The parasite, which has sickened thousands across the U.S., is notoriously difficult to track. Funding cuts haven’t made the job easier.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Hopkins Legaspi
item.media:credit: Jae Young Ju/Getty Images

item.title: How to Stay Safe From Cyclospora, a Parasite Causing Severe Intestinal Illness
item.description: Cyclospora, a parasite that causes intestinal illness, is sickening many people across the United States. Health experts have tips for avoiding infection.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Yana Iskayeva/Getty Images

item.title: As Confirmation Hearing Nears, Todd Blanche’s Role in Epstein Case Draws Scrutiny
item.description: Todd Blanche’s role in interviewing Ghislaine Maxwell, a longtime associate of the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, will be front and center as he seeks to become attorney general.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, last month. As the Justice Department’s No. 2 official, he oversaw the release of the Epstein files.

item.title: Todd Blanche Faces Crucial Hurdle Ahead of Confirmation as Attorney General
item.description: Todd Blanche’s nomination is broadly a referendum on President Trump’s vision of the Justice Department as a projection of his power.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Devlin Barrett and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, during a House hearing last month.

item.title: Paramedics Help Save Lives in New York. Can They Afford to Live There?
item.description: Some of New York City’s emergency medical workers make just $18 an hour. Their boss, Zohran Mamdani, has staked his mayoralty on making New York City more affordable for all.
item.dc:creator: Eliza Shapiro and Vincent Alban
item.media:description: Taysha Soto has spent the last two years in close touch with life and death in New York City as an emergency medical technician.

item.title: Mamdani Will Advance Bronx Bus Lane Project to Improve Transit Speeds
item.description: The road redesign to prioritize buses, the first such project in the borough, had been put on hold after the Trump administration objected to a similar plan in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack and Stefanos Chen
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: In addition to a dedicated “busway” on Tremont Avenue in the Bronx, the Mamdani administration will gather public input on plans to improve bus service on major roads in Brooklyn.

item.title: He Has a $25 Million Bounty on His Head, But Is Also a U.S. Partner in Venezuela.
item.description: A list of alleged crimes has not prevented Diosdado Cabello, and other Venezuelan officials, from working with a U.S. government that once hounded them.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Diosdado Cabello, Venezuela’s interior minister, welcomed a flight last year bringing Venezuelan migrants from Mexico.

item.title: Senator Mark Kelly Amasses Nearly $25 Million Campaign War Chest
item.description: The Arizona Democrat said he had raised and given away $10 million to fellow members of his party and committees as he mulls a presidential run in 2028.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mark Kelly’s fund-raising jumped last year after President Trump accused him and five other Democrats of “sedition” over a video in which they told service members they could ignore illegal orders.

item.title: Platner Was Toxic. But Democrats Could Learn From His Politics.
item.description: His progressive, populist message was able to occupy a kind of middle ground in the party’s primary electorate.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Should Epstein’s Friends Be Punished? An Elite Harvard Club Is Torn.
item.description: At Harvard, students, alumni and professors debate whether a storied campus group should cut ties with one of Jeffrey Epstein’s closest associates, and whether Harvard itself should do the same.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Jemal Countess/Getty Images for The Hasty Pudding Institute of 1770
item.media:description: Andrew Farkas spoke in 2014 at an event for the Hasty Pudding Institute, a Harvard group. Mr. Farkas has come under criticism for soliciting funds for the group from Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: The American E.V. Has Been Crushed. Will It Take the U.S. Auto Industry With It?
item.description: The largest U.S. automakers have backed away from electric vehicles, even as global sales are booming. The decision may make them obsolete.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Shaer

item.title: The Senate Should Reject Todd Blanche
item.description: Republicans should have the decency, patriotism and self-respect to vote against Mr. Blanche.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Pete Keihart for The New York Times

item.title: ‘It’s Chaos’: What New York’s Suburbs Can Tell Us About the Mood of the Country
item.description: The midterms are looming. We asked people in New York City’s suburbs how they feel about the state of America.
item.dc:creator: Mara Gay and Emily Holzknecht

item.title: Late Night Calls Lindsey Graham’s Senate Seat ‘an Inheritance Jackpot’
item.description: “A Senate seat? Most people just get a garage full of damp boxes,” Michael Kosta said of Graham’s sister filling the late senator’s seat on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Alzheimer’s Blood Tests Offer New Promise to Diagnose and Predict the Disease
item.description: New studies are shedding light on how the tests could eventually transform treatment.
item.dc:creator: Dana G. Smith and Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs via Getty
